chunk_summary: This section discusses managing borderline anthropometric values (e.g., MUAC 124–125 mm, WHZ near -2) and discordant metrics in outpatient care, emphasizing risk stratification, follow-up, and program eligibility criteria.
learning_objectives: Understand the significance of borderline MUAC and WHZ values in clinical risk assessment.
Learn technical steps to confirm anthropometric measurements and address discordance.
Identify WHO risk factors for prioritizing intensive dietary support and follow-up.
Explore strategies for managing children with borderline metrics in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Borderline MUAC values: 124–125 mm; WHZ near -2 indicate overlapping but distinct risk phenotypes.
Mortality risk is highest when both MUAC and WHZ are low.
WHO risk factors include MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age < 24 months, comorbidities (e.g., HIV, TB), and severe social circumstances.
Technical confirmation involves repeating measurements, calibrating equipment, and ensuring proper technique.
In clinics without specialized foods, tighter follow-up and linkage to supplementary feeding are recommended.
reference_quote: Mortality risk is highest when both MUAC and WHZ are low. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK601642/)
document_key_topics: Discordance between MUAC and WHZ metrics
WHO risk factors for dietary support prioritization
Follow-up strategies in resource-limited outpatient care
